hero.headline: What test does Michigan use to classify workers?
hero.subhead: Three frameworks. One wrong answer. No ABC test.
hero.partner_badge: Served via Teamed partner: Suna Solutions (US payroll partner)

answer_capsule.text: Michigan classifies workers under three separate frameworks: the IRS 20-factor common-law control test (MCL 421.42, effective 1st January 2013) for Unemployment Insurance, the IRS three-category control test for income-tax withholding, and the Michigan Construction Industry Worker Classification Act (2017) for the construction sector. No ABC test exists in Michigan. Proposed Senate Bills 6 and 7 would add an intent-based penalty structure, including up to a $10,000 fine and a two-year felony for repeat offenders.

classification_framework.question: What test does Michigan use to classify workers as employees?
classification_framework.answer_first: Michigan uses three separate tests depending on context. There's no single universal classification rule.
classification_framework.body:
For Unemployment Insurance (UI) purposes, Michigan adopted the IRS 20-factor common-law control test effective 1st January 2013, codified at MCL 421.42 (Michigan Employment Security Act). The test derives directly from IRS Revenue Ruling 87-41. Any worker an employer is required to withhold federal income tax from is prima facie an employee under the Michigan Employment Security Act.

For income-tax withholding, the relevant framework is the IRS three-category control test: Behavioural Control (instructions, training), Financial Control (investment, profit/loss, opportunity, methods of payment), and Type of Relationship (contracts, benefits, permanency, integral services).

For the construction sector, a third framework applies: the Michigan Construction Industry Worker Classification Act (2017), which sets specific criteria for construction contractors and subcontractors.

Michigan has no ABC test. Senate Bills 6 and 7 (reintroduced 2026) would create one, but neither has been enacted as of 30th May 2026.

Run the contractor classifier now to map your worker's profile against all three frameworks.

framework_table.test: Michigan Construction Industry Worker Classification Act

twenty_factor_test.question: What are the 20 factors Michigan uses for unemployment insurance?
twenty_factor_test.answer_first: Michigan UIA applies IRS Revenue Ruling 87-41's 20-factor test. No single factor is decisive.
twenty_factor_test.body:
The 20 factors group into three broad questions: who controls the work method, who bears the financial risk, and what kind of relationship exists. Here's the full list Michigan UIA applies.

The factors are: (1) Instructions — a worker required to comply with instructions about when, where, and how to work is generally an employee. (2) Training — required training suggests employee status. (3) Integration — services integral to core business operations suggest employment. (4) Services rendered personally — requirement to perform personally suggests employment. (5) Hiring/supervising/paying assistants — if the hiring party does so, the worker is likely an employee. (6) Continuing relationship — an ongoing relationship suggests employment. (7) Set hours of work — employer-set hours indicate employment. (8) Full-time required — if full-time work is required, the worker is likely an employee. (9) Doing work on premises — working on the payer's premises suggests employment. (10) Order or sequence set — required sequence/order suggests employment. (11) Oral or written reports — required progress reports suggest employment. (12) Payment by the hour, week, or month — periodic payment suggests employment. (13) Payment of expenses — if the payer pays business/travel expenses, employment is likely. (14) Furnishing tools/materials — supplying tools/equipment suggests employment. (15) Significant investment — a significant investment in facilities by the worker suggests independent contractor status. (16) Realising profit or loss — risk of profit or loss suggests independent contractor status. (17) Working for multiple firms simultaneously — working for multiple unrelated firms suggests independent contractor status. (18) Making services available to the general public — marketing availability to the general public suggests independent contractor status. (19) Right to discharge — the right to fire without contractual consequences suggests employment. (20) Right to terminate — if the worker can quit without liability, they are likely an employee.

No single factor controls. A named Teamed Legal Operations specialist maps every engagement against all 20 before placing a Michigan worker.

penalties.question: What are the penalties for misclassifying workers in Michigan?
penalties.answer_first: Current penalties include back taxes, civil fines, and criminal exposure. Proposed 2026 legislation would add felony charges.
penalties.body:
Michigan's current misclassification consequences run across four enforcement channels.

First, unpaid Unemployment Insurance taxes. If a worker you classified as a contractor is reclassified as an employee by the UIA, you owe all back UI contributions plus interest and penalties.

Second, workers' compensation liability. If a misclassified worker is injured, you're responsible for all workers' comp benefits — as though they were always on payroll. Failing to carry required workers' compensation insurance is a criminal misdemeanor carrying up to six months imprisonment and a $1,000 per day fine.

Third, civil fines under the Improved Workforce Opportunity Wage Act — up to $1,000 for wage-related misclassification violations.

Fourth, the proposed pathway. Senate Bills 6 and 7 (reintroduced 2026, not yet enacted) would introduce: a $1,000 fine and one-year misdemeanor for first-time intentional misclassification; a $10,000 fine and two-year felony for repeat violations; and felony charges of 10–15 years for failure to pay wages of $20,000 or more. As of 30th May 2026, these are proposed, not law.

One Teamed EOR engagement eliminates the classification question entirely — the worker is an employee from day one.

proposed_penalties.violation: Repeat violation with intent to defraud (SBs 6 & 7)

penalties.prompt_correction_note: Cache prompt asserted '$10,000 per worker' — verified research shows $10,000 is per-repeat-violation (not per worker) and is proposed legislation (SBs 6 & 7), not enacted law.

construction_sector.question: How does the Michigan construction worker classification work?
construction_sector.answer_first: Construction workers face a separate Michigan statute from 2017. The 20-factor UI test doesn't apply here.
construction_sector.body:
The Michigan Construction Industry Worker Classification Act (effective 2017) creates a distinct classification regime for the construction sector. If you're engaging workers on Michigan construction projects, the IRS 20-factor test used for UI is not your primary reference — this sector-specific statute is.

The Act establishes criteria for determining whether a construction worker or subcontractor is a bona fide independent contractor. A worker is more likely to be considered an independent contractor in construction if they: hold a valid construction contractor licence; have a separate business entity; provide their own tools and equipment; control the method and manner of their own work; operate under a written contract; are free to work for multiple clients; and bear their own business risk.

Misclassification in construction carries the same back-tax and workers' comp liability as in other sectors, but the construction sector historically faces the highest audit rates in Michigan.

Teamed's construction payroll specialists understand both the CIWCA and the UI 20-factor overlap. Ask your Teamed Legal Operations contact before placing any Michigan construction worker.

eor_context.question: How does an EOR remove Michigan worker classification risk?
eor_context.answer_first: An EOR makes all three Michigan tests answer themselves. Every worker is an employee from day one.
eor_context.body:
Michigan's tri-framework problem — three tests, three sets of penalties, one worker — is the structural reason EOR exists for this market. When Teamed acts as Employer of Record for your Michigan hire, the IRS 20-factor common-law test for UI, the three-category withholding test, and the construction-sector statute all resolve in the same direction: the worker is Teamed's employee on the books, not your contractor.

You keep the working relationship. You direct the work, set the schedule, and manage the output. Teamed carries the Michigan Employment Security Act exposure, the workers' comp obligation, and the UI contribution. You get one platform, one invoice, and Zero FX on the payroll line.

Teamed runs Michigan payroll via Suna Solutions (US payroll partner). The Teamed service fee is $599 per employee per month, flat, in any currency pairing. All statutory costs — Michigan UI contributions (2.7% new employer rate, $9,000 wage base), workers' comp, ESTA sick leave accrual — pass through at cost, itemised on every invoice.

When your Michigan headcount justifies your own US entity, Teamed tells you. That's the graduation model — EOR until it isn't.

good_to_know.question: What should you know about Michigan's classification landscape before hiring?
good_to_know.body:
Michigan is one of the US states where the absence of an ABC test is itself a trap. Most compliance teams check 'does this state have an ABC test?' and then stop reading. Michigan's answer is no — but two other tests still run in parallel, and a third applies if your worker builds anything.

The 2026 legislative session has live bills (SBs 6 & 7) that would change the criminal penalty structure materially. Michigan has also changed its sick leave law twice in two years (PMLA to ESTA, effective 21st February 2025), so any playbook from 2023 is out of date.

For US tech and creative workers specifically, the risk sits in the 'integration' factor (factor 3 of the 20-factor test): if the work is core to your product, the UIA is likely to view the worker as an employee regardless of contract language.

faq.q: Does Michigan use the ABC test for worker classification?
faq.a: No. Michigan does not have an ABC test as of 30th May 2026. The state uses the IRS 20-factor common-law control test (MCL 421.42) for Unemployment Insurance and the IRS three-category control test for income-tax withholding. Senate Bills 6 and 7, reintroduced in 2026, would introduce a stricter test but have not been enacted.

faq.q: What is the Michigan 20-factor test for worker classification?
faq.a: Michigan adopted the IRS 20-factor test from Revenue Ruling 87-41 effective 1st January 2013 under MCL 421.42. The factors cover instructions, training, integration, personal service requirement, control over assistants, continuity of relationship, set hours, full-time requirement, work location, sequence of work, reporting, payment method, expenses, tools, worker investment, profit/loss risk, multi-firm work, public availability, right to discharge, and right to terminate.

faq.q: What are the penalties for worker misclassification in Michigan?
faq.a: Current law: back UI contributions plus interest, workers' comp liability including a $1,000-per-day fine for lack of coverage, and civil fines up to $1,000 under the Improved Workforce Opportunity Wage Act. Proposed Senate Bills 6 and 7 (not yet enacted) would add a $10,000 fine and two-year felony for repeat intentional misclassification.

faq.q: Does the Michigan construction worker classification work differently?
faq.a: Yes. The Michigan Construction Industry Worker Classification Act (2017) creates a separate classification regime for construction workers. The IRS 20-factor UI test is not the primary framework for construction. Construction workers face the highest misclassification audit rates in Michigan.

faq.q: How does an Employer of Record help with Michigan worker classification?
faq.a: An Employer of Record places the worker on payroll as a direct employee from day one, making all three Michigan classification tests resolve in favour of employment. You direct the work; Teamed carries the Michigan UI exposure, workers' comp obligation, and ESTA sick leave accrual at $599 per employee per month, flat, Zero FX.
